**Section 21 \- Digital Learning and Technology Infrastructure**

## **Purpose** {#purpose}

This section establishes a comprehensive framework for effective, secure, and pedagogically sound use of technology across all Eduversal Partner Schools. It ensures that digital learning tools, infrastructure, and policies support high-quality teaching and learning while maintaining cybersecurity, data protection, and online safety standards. The framework aligns with Indonesian cybersecurity regulations, Cambridge Assessment International Education's digital guidelines, and CIS/WASC standards for learning environments and technology integration.

## 

## **21.1 Digital Learning Vision and Strategy** {#21.1-digital-learning-vision-and-strategy}

### **21.1.1 Vision Statement** {#21.1.1-vision-statement}

Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to leveraging technology to enhance teaching and learning, prepare students for success in a digital world, and develop responsible digital citizens. Technology serves as a tool to support pedagogical goals, not as an end in itself. All technology integration must demonstrate clear educational benefit and align with the school's Schoolwide Learner Outcomes (SLOs), particularly Innovation and Communication.

### **21.1.2 Strategic Objectives** {#21.1.2-strategic-objectives}

The Digital Learning Strategy aims to achieve the following objectives over a 3-5 year horizon:

**Teaching and Learning Enhancement:** Integrate technology meaningfully into curriculum delivery to personalize learning, increase engagement, support diverse learners (including EAL students), and develop 21st-century skills aligned with Cambridge International standards and Indonesian national curriculum requirements.

**Infrastructure Development:** Maintain robust, reliable, and secure IT infrastructure that supports teaching and learning activities, administrative operations, and communication needs across all school contexts (day schools, boarding schools, urban and rural settings).

**Digital Literacy and Citizenship:** Develop students' digital literacy skills and responsible technology use, preparing them to be ethical and effective digital citizens who understand online safety, privacy, and the responsible use of emerging technologies including artificial intelligence.

**Staff Professional Development:** Build staff capacity to integrate technology effectively through ongoing professional development aligned with Cambridge's professional development pathways, including "Getting Started with AI in the Classroom" and "Teaching with Technology" modules.

**Data Security and Privacy:** Protect student, staff, and institutional data through robust cybersecurity measures, compliance with Indonesian data protection regulations (UU PDP), and adherence to Cambridge secure materials handling requirements.

### **21.1.3 Alignment with School Improvement** {#21.1.3-alignment-with-school-improvement}

Digital learning initiatives are embedded within the School Improvement Plan (SIP) and monitored through EduNav dashboards. Technology-related KPIs are tracked as part of the school appraisal process under Domain 4: Educational Resources and Technology.

*Cross-Reference: See Section 4 for Strategic Planning and School Improvement framework.*

## **21.2 EduNav Platform: School Information System and Learning Management System** {#21.2-edunav-platform:-school-information-system-and-learning-management-system}

### **21.2.1 Overview of EduNav** {#21.2.1-overview-of-edunav}

EduNav is the integrated School Information System (SIS) and Learning Management System (LMS) deployed across all Eduversal Partner Schools. Developed and maintained by PT. Global Zerone Digital (Zerone), EduNav serves as the central digital platform for school operations, teaching delivery, and stakeholder communication.

EduNav provides accounts for students, teachers, staff, and parents with customized access to relevant educational services. The platform supports student information management, learning evaluations, attendance tracking, grading, scheduling, and school administration.

### **21.2.2 Core Functions** {#21.2.2-core-functions}

**Student Information System (SIS) Functions:**

* Student enrollment and registration management  
* Attendance tracking and absence reporting  
* Gradebook and academic record management  
* Scheduling and timetable management  
* Parent communication and notification systems  
* Administrative reporting and data analytics  
* Integration with Indonesian Dapodik requirements

**Learning Management System (LMS) Functions:**

* Course content organization and delivery  
* Assignment creation, submission, and feedback  
* Online quizzes and assessments  
* Discussion forums and collaborative tools  
* Resource library and digital content repository  
* Progress tracking and learning analytics  
* Communication tools (announcements, messaging)

### 

### **21.2.3 User Access and Roles** {#21.2.3-user-access-and-roles}

EduNav implements role-based access control to ensure users can only access information appropriate to their role:

**Students:** Access to personal academic records, course materials, assignments, grades, attendance records, and communication tools. Students can submit work, participate in discussions, and view feedback.

**Teachers:** Access to class rosters, gradebook functionality, course management tools, communication features, and student academic information for assigned classes. Teachers are required to use EduNav-LMS exclusively for lesson delivery; use of alternative platforms is prohibited without explicit approval.

**Parents/Guardians:** Access to their child's academic progress, grades, attendance, behavior records, and school communications. Parents receive automated notifications for significant assessments and communications.

**School Administrators:** Full access to administrative functions, reporting tools, and system configuration. Access to student records is governed by need-to-know principles.

**Eduversal Central:** Oversight access for quality assurance, appraisal data, and network-wide reporting while respecting data protection requirements.

### **21.2.4 Teacher Responsibilities in EduNav** {#21.2.4-teacher-responsibilities-in-edunav}

Teachers have specific responsibilities for effective use of the LMS:

* Maintain current and organized course content aligned with curriculum standards  
* Provide clear directions and instructions when sharing materials  
* Update gradebook regularly with assessment results and feedback  
* Encourage students to use comment features for communication  
* Respond to student queries within reasonable timeframes  
* Participate in training programs for LMS usage  
* Report technical issues promptly to IT support

### 

### **21.2.5 Data Quality Assurance** {#21.2.5-data-quality-assurance}

Schools ensure data integrity through regular audits and quality checks:

* Beginning of term: Enrollment and schedule verification  
* Mid-term: Gradebook compliance check  
* End of term: Final grade accuracy audit  
* Annual: Comprehensive system review

*Cross-Reference: See Section 6.7 for Gradebook configuration and assessment reporting; Section 14 for Data Protection policies.*

## 

## **21.3 IT Infrastructure and Device Management** {#21.3-it-infrastructure-and-device-management}

### **21.3.1 Network Infrastructure** {#21.3.1-network-infrastructure}

Schools maintain robust network infrastructure to support digital learning:

**Internet Connectivity:**

* Sufficient bandwidth for simultaneous use by students and staff  
* Redundant connections where feasible for continuity  
* Content filtering at network level (see Section 21.5)  
* Guest network for visitors separate from educational network

**Wireless Network:**

* Campus-wide Wi-Fi coverage including classrooms, common areas, and boarding facilities  
* Secure authentication for authorized users  
* Regular network performance monitoring  
* Credentials shared securely (posted in appropriate areas for authorized users)

**Server Infrastructure:**

* On-premises servers for local applications (where applicable)  
* Cloud-based services for EduNav and backup systems  
* Regular maintenance schedules and update protocols  
* Disaster recovery procedures

### **21.3.2 Classroom Technology** {#21.3.2-classroom-technology}

Standard classroom technology provision includes:

**Interactive Flat Panels (IFPs):** Interactive Flat Panels serve as digital whiteboards enabling teachers to present material and engage students in interactive activities. IFPs feature:

* Large touch-sensitive displays for visual instruction  
* Multi-user capability allowing collaborative work  
* Built-in educational software via Android Playstore  
* Screen mirroring from teacher devices  
* Annotation and recording capabilities

Teachers receive training on effective IFP use to enhance visual learning and student engagement.

**Projectors and Display Equipment:** Where IFPs are not available, schools provide projectors and screens for classroom presentation.

### **21.3.3 Device Ecosystem** {#21.3.3-device-ecosystem}

Eduversal Partner Schools utilize a managed device ecosystem:

**iPads:** iPads serve as primary digital learning tools offering:

* Wide range of educational apps and resources  
* Touch screen interface for intuitive interaction  
* Portability for flexible learning environments  
* Camera and multimedia capabilities for project work  
* Accessibility features for diverse learners

**Chromebooks:** Chromebooks provide cost-effective options for:

* Web-based learning activities  
* Document creation and collaboration  
* Assessment completion  
* Research and information access

**Teacher Devices:** Teachers are provided with devices (typically iPads) to:

* Manage student devices during lessons  
* Access classroom management applications  
* Deliver content through IFP or projectors  
* Track attendance and record grades  
* Communicate with students and parents

### 

### **21.3.4 Mobile Device Management (MDM)** {#21.3.4-mobile-device-management-(mdm)}

All school-owned devices are enrolled in Mobile Device Management systems:

**Jamf (for Apple Devices):** Jamf provides comprehensive MDM capabilities for iPads and other Apple devices:

* Device enrollment and configuration  
* App deployment and management  
* Content distribution  
* Classroom management policies  
* Remote monitoring and control  
* Security policy enforcement  
* Lost device tracking and remote wipe

**Apple Classroom App:** Integrated with Jamf, the Classroom app enables teachers to:

* Guide students to specific apps or websites  
* Lock student screens to maintain focus  
* View student screens for monitoring  
* Share documents and content  
* Reset forgotten passwords

**Securly (for Chromebooks):** Securly provides web filtering and classroom management for Chromebooks:

* Web content filtering  
* Screen monitoring during lessons  
* Collaboration tools  
* Browsing behavior reports  
* Online safety alerts

### 

### **21.3.5 Device Care and Responsibilities** {#21.3.5-device-care-and-responsibilities}

**Student Responsibilities:**

* Handle devices with care and respect  
* Store devices in designated locations when not in use  
* Report damage or malfunction immediately  
* Use only authorized applications and websites  
* Follow charging and storage procedures  
* Do not remove school property from campus without permission

**Teacher Responsibilities:**

* Always bring and use teacher device during lessons  
* Monitor student device use throughout instruction  
* Ensure devices are returned to charging stations  
* Report damage or issues promptly  
* Model appropriate device use

**School Responsibilities:**

* Provide adequate charging infrastructure  
* Maintain device inventory and tracking  
* Schedule regular maintenance and updates  
* Provide protective cases and accessories  
* Budget for replacement and repair

### **21.3.6 Device Usage During Lessons** {#21.3.6-device-usage-during-lessons}

Teachers must actively manage device usage:

* Students should not use digital devices before teachers direct them to do so  
* Teachers use video lessons only in "live mode" to prevent unauthorized browsing  
* Teachers must always monitor student screens during lessons using Securly (Chromebook) or Jamf/Classroom (iPad)  
* Be cautious of students using earphones/headphones as they may be engaged in off-task activities  
* No tolerance for rule violations regarding device use in the classroom

### 

### **21.3.7 Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) Policy** {#21.3.7-bring-your-own-device-(byod)-policy}

Where schools permit personal devices:

**General Guidelines:**

* Personal devices may be permitted with parent consent and registration  
* Must comply with school network security requirements  
* Subject to same acceptable use policies as school devices  
* School not responsible for damage, loss, or theft  
* May be restricted to specific times or activities

**Registration Requirements:**

* Device details recorded with IT department  
* Security software installation may be required  
* Agreement to monitoring during school hours  
* Compliance acknowledgment signed

*Cross-Reference: See Section 22.8.3 for mobile phone confiscation policy.*

## 

## **21.4 Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)** {#21.4-acceptable-use-policy-(aup)}

### **21.4.1 Purpose and Scope** {#21.4.1-purpose-and-scope}

The Acceptable Use Policy establishes guidelines for the appropriate use of school technology resources by all community members. The AUP applies to:

* All school-owned devices and equipment  
* Personal devices used on school networks  
* School network and internet access  
* School-provided accounts and systems  
* Technology use during school-related activities (including trips)  
* Online behavior affecting the school community

### **21.4.2 Student Acceptable Use Policy** {#21.4.2-student-acceptable-use-policy}

**Permitted Uses:**

* Educational activities assigned or approved by teachers  
* Research using appropriate sources  
* Communication through school-approved platforms  
* Creative projects supporting curriculum objectives  
* Collaboration with classmates on school work

**Prohibited Uses:**

* Accessing inappropriate content (violent, sexual, illegal, or harmful material)  
* Cyberbullying, harassment, or threatening communications  
* Sharing or viewing others' passwords or accounts  
* Attempting to bypass security or filtering systems  
* Downloading unauthorized software or files  
* Using technology for cheating or academic dishonesty  
* Recording others without consent  
* Sharing personal information of self or others online  
* Accessing social media during instructional time (unless directed by teacher)  
* Gaming during school hours without teacher permission  
* Any illegal activity

**AI Tools:** Students may use artificial intelligence tools only within teacher-approved guidelines. Unauthorized or undisclosed AI use in assessments constitutes academic dishonesty. When AI use is permitted, students must:

* Only use approved AI tools as specified by the teacher  
* Disclose AI assistance in their work  
* Maintain original thinking and personal voice  
* Not submit AI-generated content as their own work

*Cross-Reference: See Section 6.4 and Section 22.8.2 for academic integrity policies regarding AI use.*

### **21.4.3 Staff Acceptable Use Policy** {#21.4.3-staff-acceptable-use-policy}

**Professional Use:**

* Technology use should primarily support teaching, learning, and administration  
* Personal use during working hours should be minimal and not interfere with duties  
* Staff must model appropriate technology use for students  
* All communications through school systems may be monitored

**Prohibited Uses:**

* Sharing student information through unauthorized channels  
* Using school resources for personal commercial purposes  
* Accessing or sharing inappropriate content  
* Installing unauthorized software on school devices  
* Sharing login credentials with others  
* Connecting unauthorized devices to school networks  
* Using personal social media to communicate with students

**Social Media Professional Guidelines:**

* Maintain professional boundaries in all online interactions  
* Clearly distinguish personal opinions from school positions  
* Protect privacy and confidentiality of students and colleagues  
* Avoid forming personal relationships with students on social media  
* Communication with students should primarily occur through official school channels  
* Do not share sensitive or private school information

### 

### **21.4.4 Parent/Guardian Access and Responsibilities** {#21.4.4-parent/guardian-access-and-responsibilities}

**EduNav Access:**

* Use credentials securely and do not share  
* Access only information related to own child  
* Contact school for access issues

**Communication:**

* Use official channels for school communication  
* Respect staff response time expectations  
* Report concerns about technology safety promptly

### **21.4.5 AUP Agreement Process** {#21.4.5-aup-agreement-process}

**Annual Agreement:**

* Students and parents sign AUP acknowledgment at enrollment and annually  
* Staff sign AUP acknowledgment upon employment and annually  
* Signed agreements maintained on file

**Communication:**

* AUP published in Student and Parent Handbook (bilingual)  
* Explained during orientation for new students and families  
* Regular reinforcement through assemblies and lessons  
* Age-appropriate versions for younger students

### 

### 

### **21.4.6 Consequences for AUP Violations** {#21.4.6-consequences-for-aup-violations}

Violations of the AUP are addressed through the school's discipline framework:

**Minor Violations (Level 1):** Verbal warning, educational conversation, temporary loss of technology privileges

**Moderate Violations (Level 2):** Written warning, parent notification, suspension of technology privileges, detention

**Serious Violations (Level 3):** Formal warning letter, extended loss of privileges, possible suspension

**Severe Violations (Level 4):** May result in long-term suspension or expulsion, police referral if illegal activity involved

*Cross-Reference: See Section 22 for discipline procedures and consequences framework.*

## 

## **21.5 Internet Safety and Content Filtering** {#21.5-internet-safety-and-content-filtering}

### **21.5.1 Content Filtering Strategy** {#21.5.1-content-filtering-strategy}

Schools implement multi-layer content filtering to protect students from harmful content:

**Network-Level Filtering:**

* All internet traffic filtered through approved systems  
* Category-based blocking (violence, adult content, gambling, etc.)  
* URL blacklists for known harmful sites  
* Real-time threat protection  
* SSL inspection where appropriate

**Device-Level Filtering:**

* MDM policies restrict app installation  
* Browser restrictions on managed devices  
* Securly filtering on Chromebooks  
* Apple Screen Time and restrictions on iPads

### **21.5.2 Monitoring and Reporting** {#21.5.2-monitoring-and-reporting}

**Activity Monitoring:**

* Web browsing history logged for managed devices  
* Alerts generated for concerning search terms or sites  
* Teacher visibility into student screens during lessons  
* Reports available for pattern analysis

**Safety Alerts:**

* Automated alerts for high-risk content or behavior  
* Immediate notification to designated staff  
* Escalation procedures for serious concerns  
* Integration with safeguarding procedures

### 

### **21.5.3 Search and Device Inspection** {#21.5.3-search-and-device-inspection}

Schools reserve the right to inspect devices for policy compliance:

**School Devices:**

* May be inspected at any time without notice  
* Content considered school property  
* Students have no expectation of privacy on school devices

**Personal Devices (on School Network):**

* Subject to AUP when connected to school network  
* May be required to show screens when requested  
* Content inspection requires reasonable suspicion and administrator approval

*Cross-Reference: See Section 22.8.4 for Search and Seizure policy.*

### **21.5.4 Cyberbullying Prevention and Response** {#21.5.4-cyberbullying-prevention-and-response}

**Definition:** Cyberbullying includes harassment, threats, humiliation, or intimidation through digital technologies including social media, messaging, email, online gaming, and other digital platforms.

**Prevention:**

* Digital citizenship education in curriculum  
* Clear expectations in AUP  
* Positive bystander training  
* Anonymous reporting mechanisms  
* Peer support programs

**Response:**

* Report to homeroom teacher, counselor, or SC Coordinator  
* Immediate intervention to stop ongoing harassment  
* Investigation following discipline procedures  
* Support for targets  
* Consequences for perpetrators (minimum Level 3\)  
* Parent notification for all parties  
* Documentation and monitoring

*Cross-Reference: See Section 9.6 for cyberbullying within safeguarding framework; Section 22.8.1 for anti-bullying policy.*

## **21.6 Digital Citizenship Education** {#21.6-digital-citizenship-education}

### **21.6.1 Curriculum Integration** {#21.6.1-curriculum-integration}

Digital citizenship is taught explicitly and integrated across subjects:

**Key Topics:**

* Responsible use of technology  
* Online communication etiquette  
* Protecting personal information and privacy  
* Respecting intellectual property (copyright, citation)  
* Understanding consequences of online behavior  
* Recognizing and reporting online dangers  
* Critical evaluation of online information  
* Digital footprint and reputation management  
* Healthy technology habits and screen time balance

### **21.6.2 Age-Appropriate Instruction** {#21.6.2-age-appropriate-instruction}

**Primary (Years 1-6):**

* Basic internet safety (strangers online, personal information)  
* Kind communication online  
* Asking adults for help with technology problems  
* Taking breaks from screens  
* Understanding that not everything online is true

**Lower Secondary (Years 7-8):**

* Social media awareness and safety  
* Cyberbullying recognition and response  
* Privacy settings and digital footprint  
* Basic copyright and citation  
* Recognizing credible sources  
* Peer pressure online

**Upper Secondary (Years 9-12):**

* Advanced digital literacy and source evaluation  
* Social media impact on future opportunities  
* Legal aspects of online behavior  
* AI literacy and ethical use  
* Data privacy and security  
* Digital wellbeing and balance  
* Professional online presence

### **21.6.3 Parent Education** {#21.6.3-parent-education}

Schools provide parent education on digital citizenship:

* Information sessions on online safety  
* Resources on parental controls and monitoring  
* Guidance on managing screen time at home  
* Updates on emerging technology trends and risks  
* Communication about school digital policies

*Cross-Reference: See Section 16 for parent engagement programs.*

## 

## **21.7 Cybersecurity Policy** {#21.7-cybersecurity-policy}

### **21.7.1 Security Framework** {#21.7.1-security-framework}

Schools implement comprehensive cybersecurity measures aligned with Indonesian cybersecurity regulations:

**Preventive Controls:**

* Firewall and intrusion prevention systems  
* Antivirus and anti-malware protection  
* Regular security patching and updates  
* Strong password policies  
* Multi-factor authentication where appropriate  
* Email security and spam filtering  
* Data encryption in transit and at rest

**Detective Controls:**

* Network monitoring and logging  
* Security information and event management  
* Regular vulnerability assessments  
* User activity auditing  
* Anomaly detection systems

**Corrective Controls:**

* Incident response procedures  
* Backup and recovery systems  
* Business continuity planning  
* Security awareness training

### 

### **21.7.2 Password and Account Security** {#21.7.2-password-and-account-security}

**Password Requirements:**

* Minimum 8 characters (12+ for administrators)  
* Combination of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols  
* Regular password changes (90 days for staff, annually for students)  
* No password sharing under any circumstances  
* No reuse of previous passwords

**Account Management:**

* Unique accounts for each user  
* Immediate deactivation upon departure  
* Access reviews conducted termly  
* Privileged access limited and monitored

### **21.7.3 Data Backup and Recovery** {#21.7.3-data-backup-and-recovery}

**Backup Procedures:**

* Daily automated backups of critical systems  
* Multiple backup locations (local and cloud)  
* Regular backup testing and verification  
* Documented recovery procedures  
* Recovery time objectives defined

**Data Retention:**

* Assessment data: Per retention schedule (see Section 14\)  
* System logs: Minimum 90 days  
* Backup archives: Per regulatory requirements

### 

### **21.7.4 Incident Response** {#21.7.4-incident-response}

**Incident Categories:**

* Category 1 (Critical): Data breach involving personal information, ransomware, complete system failure  
* Category 2 (High): Security compromise, significant service disruption, malware infection  
* Category 3 (Medium): Policy violations, minor security incidents, isolated failures  
* Category 4 (Low): Suspicious activity, minor technical issues

**Response Procedures:**

1. Detection and initial assessment  
2. Containment and isolation  
3. Notification to relevant parties (IT Coordinator, Principal, Eduversal Central for Category 1-2)  
4. Investigation and evidence preservation  
5. Eradication and recovery  
6. Post-incident review and documentation  
7. Lessons learned and policy updates

**Data Breach Response:** In case of data breach involving personal information:

* Immediate notification to Principal and Eduversal Central  
* Assessment of scope and impact  
* Notification to affected parties per regulatory requirements  
* Report to Indonesian data protection authorities if required  
* Remediation measures implementation  
* Documentation and review

*Cross-Reference: See Section 15 for Risk Management and Emergency Preparedness.*

## 

## **21.8 Artificial Intelligence Policy** {#21.8-artificial-intelligence-policy}

### **21.8.1 Vision for AI in Education** {#21.8.1-vision-for-ai-in-education}

Eduversal recognizes artificial intelligence as an emerging tool that can significantly enhance teaching and learning when used ethically and responsibly. AI should complement, not replace, quality teaching and genuine student learning. The school's approach to AI balances embracing beneficial applications with maintaining academic integrity and safeguarding student development.

### **21.8.2 Permitted Uses of AI** {#21.8.2-permitted-uses-of-ai}

**For Teachers:**

* Lesson planning assistance and resource creation  
* Differentiation and personalization support  
* Administrative task automation (with appropriate review)  
* Assessment design (not for grading without teacher review)  
* Professional learning and research  
* Communication drafting (with appropriate review)

Teachers must always review and verify AI-generated content before use with students.

**For Students (When Explicitly Permitted by Teacher):**

* Research assistance and information synthesis  
* Brainstorming and idea generation  
* Revision and study support  
* Language learning assistance (for EAL students)  
* Creative inspiration (not final submission)  
* Coding assistance for technology projects

### 

### **21.8.3 Prohibited Uses of AI** {#21.8.3-prohibited-uses-of-ai}

**For Students:**

* Submitting AI-generated work as own work without disclosure  
* Using AI during assessments unless explicitly permitted  
* Bypassing learning processes through AI dependence  
* Using AI to complete homework or assignments intended for independent completion  
* Using AI to take tests or complete controlled assessments

**For Staff:**

* Writing student reports without substantial personal input  
* Grading student work entirely by AI without review  
* Making important decisions affecting students based solely on AI recommendations  
* Using AI that processes student personal data without proper authorization

### **21.8.4 Disclosure Requirements** {#21.8.4-disclosure-requirements}

When AI use is permitted:

* Students must acknowledge AI assistance in their work  
* Description of how AI was used (research, drafting, editing, etc.)  
* All final work must reflect student's own understanding and voice  
* Original thinking and critical analysis must be demonstrated

Failure to disclose AI assistance constitutes academic dishonesty.

### 

### **21.8.5 Teaching About AI** {#21.8.5-teaching-about-ai}

Teachers receive professional development on AI integration aligned with Cambridge's "Getting Started with AI in the Classroom" workshop:

* Understanding foundational AI concepts  
* Practical tools and strategies for effective use  
* Ethical considerations and best practices  
* Creating AI-enhanced educational environments  
* Assessment design in the AI era

Students learn about AI through age-appropriate instruction:

* Understanding what AI is and how it works  
* Recognizing AI in everyday life  
* Critical evaluation of AI-generated content  
* Ethical considerations and limitations  
* Future implications and career relevance

### **21.8.6 Academic Integrity and AI** {#21.8.6-academic-integrity-and-ai}

The relationship between AI and academic integrity is addressed through:

* Clear policy communication to students and parents  
* Age-appropriate discussions about learning value  
* Assessment design that maintains integrity  
* Detection and response procedures  
* Focus on developing genuine skills

*Cross-Reference: See Section 6.4 for academic integrity; Section 22.8.2 for AI-related misconduct consequences.*

## 

## **21.9 Educational Technology Applications** {#21.9-educational-technology-applications}

### **21.9.1 Approved Educational Applications** {#21.9.1-approved-educational-applications}

Schools utilize vetted educational applications that support curriculum delivery:

| Category | Applications | Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Video Lessons | Edpuzzle | Free |
| Interactive Presentations | Nearpod, Pear Deck | Partially Free |
| Online Collaboration | Google Docs, Slides, Sheets | Free |
| Interactive Games | Quizlet, Quizizz, Kahoot | Partially Free |
| Quizzes and Surveys | Google Forms | Free |
| Student Engagement | Seesaw, Spiral | Partially Free |
| Simulations | PhET, CK-12 | Free |
| Productivity | Pages, iBooks Author | Free (Apple) |

### **21.9.2 Application Selection Criteria** {#21.9.2-application-selection-criteria}

New educational applications are evaluated against:

* Educational value and alignment with curriculum objectives  
* Data privacy and security compliance  
* Accessibility for diverse learners (including EAL)  
* Cost-effectiveness and sustainability  
* Technical compatibility with school systems  
* Ease of use for students and teachers  
* Age-appropriateness  
* Advertising and commercial content  
* Terms of service review

### 

### **21.9.3 Application Approval Process** {#21.9.3-application-approval-process}

1. Teacher or department submits request with educational justification  
2. IT Coordinator conducts technical and security review  
3. Data Protection Impact Assessment if personal data processed  
4. Principal approval for school-wide adoption  
5. Deployment through MDM for managed devices  
6. Training provided for teachers  
7. Annual review of continued use

### **21.9.4 LMS-First Approach** {#21.9.4-lms-first-approach}

Teachers are required to use EduNav-LMS as the primary platform for lesson delivery. Alternative applications should complement, not replace, the LMS. This ensures:

* Consistency for students and parents  
* Centralized record keeping  
* Integration with assessment and reporting  
* Data security compliance

## 

## **21.10 Social Media Policy** {#21.10-social-media-policy}

### **21.10.1 Scope** {#21.10.1-scope}

This policy applies to all students, teachers, staff, parents, guardians, volunteers, and anyone representing the school in any official capacity on social media platforms, whether on-campus or off-campus.

### **21.10.2 Student Use of Social Media** {#21.10.2-student-use-of-social-media}

**Respect and Civility:** Students must maintain respectful and civil conduct when using social media. Cyberbullying, harassment, and hate speech will not be tolerated and will be addressed through discipline procedures.

**Privacy:** Students must protect their own and others' privacy by not sharing personal information such as addresses, phone numbers, or private details on social media.

**Academic Integrity:** Students must uphold academic integrity and avoid using social media for cheating, plagiarism, or any form of academic dishonesty.

**School Representation:** Students should be mindful that their online behavior reflects on the school community, particularly when identifiable through uniforms, school names, or location tags.

### **21.10.3 Staff Use of Social Media** {#21.10.3-staff-use-of-social-media}

**Professionalism:** Teachers and staff must maintain high professionalism when using social media. Personal opinions expressed online should be clearly distinguished from school positions.

**Privacy and Confidentiality:** Staff must protect the privacy and confidentiality of students, colleagues, and the school. No sensitive or private information should be shared.

**Boundaries:** Staff must avoid forming personal relationships with students on social media platforms. Communication with students should primarily occur through official school channels (EduNav, school email).

**Images and Content:** Staff must not post photos or videos of students without proper consent and must follow school media consent procedures.

### 

### **21.10.4 Official School Accounts** {#21.10.4-official-school-accounts}

**Account Ownership:** Official school social media accounts are owned and managed by the school. Only authorized staff members should have access to these accounts.

**Content Guidelines:** All content shared on official school social media accounts should:

* Align with the school's values, mission, and policies  
* Be approved by designated authority before posting  
* Comply with media consent requirements  
* Present the school positively and professionally  
* Avoid controversial or divisive content

**Monitoring:** Official accounts should be monitored regularly for comments and interactions requiring response or moderation.

## 

## **21.11 Online and Blended Learning** {#21.11-online-and-blended-learning}

### **21.11.1 Blended Learning Approach** {#21.11.1-blended-learning-approach}

Schools integrate online and face-to-face learning to enhance educational outcomes:

**Blended Learning Models:**

* Flipped classroom: Content delivered online, class time for application and discussion  
* Station rotation: Students rotate through online and face-to-face activities  
* Enrichment: Online resources extend and deepen classroom learning  
* Remediation: Online tools support students needing additional practice

**Quality Indicators:**

* Technology enhances learning objectives, not technology for its own sake  
* Balance between screen-based and other learning activities  
* Age-appropriate screen time considerations  
* Accessibility for all learners

### 

### **21.11.2 Remote/Online Learning Provisions** {#21.11.2-remote/online-learning-provisions}

When circumstances require remote learning (emergencies, health situations, extended absence):

**Teacher Responsibilities:**

1. Follow established teaching schedules for remote lessons  
2. Use approved technology tools (primarily EduNav, Google Meet/Zoom)  
3. Maintain quality and engagement comparable to in-person instruction  
4. Communicate regularly with students regarding schedules and expectations  
5. Provide clear instructions and support for technical issues  
6. Monitor student participation and follow up on non-engagement

**Student Responsibilities:**

1. Attend scheduled online sessions punctually  
2. Participate actively and appropriately  
3. Complete assignments within deadlines  
4. Communicate technical difficulties promptly  
5. Maintain appropriate behavior during online sessions  
6. Create suitable learning environment at home

**Parent Responsibilities:**

1. Ensure appropriate learning environment at home  
2. Monitor student engagement where appropriate  
3. Communicate concerns or difficulties to school  
4. Support student in maintaining routine and focus

### **21.11.3 Video Conferencing Guidelines** {#21.11.3-video-conferencing-guidelines}

For synchronous online sessions:

* Use school-approved platforms only  
* Enable waiting rooms and require authentication  
* Teachers remain in control of sessions (mute, screen share permissions)  
* Recording only with appropriate consent and notification  
* Professional backgrounds or blurred backgrounds  
* Appropriate dress code  
* Sessions scheduled during regular school hours where possible  
* Parent awareness of session schedules for younger students

## **21.12 Technology Integration in Teaching** {#21.12-technology-integration-in-teaching}

### **21.12.1 Principles of Effective Technology Integration** {#21.12.1-principles-of-effective-technology-integration}

Technology integration should follow these principles:

**Start with Learning Goals:**

* Consider how technology helps achieve specific learning objectives  
* Be intentional in choosing tools that align with instructional strategies  
* Plan technology use to support or enhance learning, not for technology's sake

**Introduce Technology Purposefully:**

* Provide clear instructions and expectations before introducing new tools  
* Model use of technology tools for students  
* Balance technology use with other learning activities

**Engage Students:**

* Use technology to create engaging and interactive learning experiences  
* Incorporate multimedia resources (videos, podcasts, simulations)  
* Encourage collaboration and communication through technology tools

**Provide Accessible and Inclusive Learning:**

* Be aware of accessibility issues for students with disabilities or special needs  
* Use Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles  
* Address the digital divide and ensure equitable access

**Choose Appropriate Tools:**

* Select tools appropriate for students' age and skill level  
* Younger students may require simpler tools  
* Consider technical support requirements

### 

### **21.12.2 Appraisal Criteria for Technology Integration** {#21.12.2-appraisal-criteria-for-technology-integration}

Technology integration is evaluated as part of school and teacher appraisal:

**LMS Usage (Domain 4.D):**

* Percentage of teachers actively using the LMS  
* Content organization and alignment with curriculum standards  
* Effective training for teachers, students, and parents

**Technology Integration in Teaching (Domain 4.E):**

* Technology resources reachable in classrooms (computers, tablets, IFPs, internet)  
* Professional development programs on technology integration  
* Technology used to enhance learning objectives and diverse instructional strategies  
* Student engagement and interaction with technology  
* Innovative practices (flipped classroom, blended learning, project-based learning)  
* Technology use in formative and summative assessments

**Digital Policy (Domain 4.F):**

* Availability and communication of digital policy  
* Digital citizenship programs  
* Policy monitoring and updates for emerging technologies

### 

### **21.12.3 Professional Development** {#21.12.3-professional-development}

Teachers receive ongoing professional development in technology integration:

**Required Training:**

* EduNav/LMS usage (during induction and annual refresher)  
* Device management (Jamf, Classroom app, Securly)  
* Digital citizenship and online safety  
* AI in the classroom

**Recommended Training (Cambridge PD):**

* Getting Started with AI in the Classroom  
* Teaching with Technology  
* Getting Started with Active Learning (technology components)

**Ongoing Support:**

* IT help desk for technical issues  
* Peer mentoring and sharing  
* Department-based collaboration  
* Access to training resources and tutorials

*Cross-Reference: See Section 12.4 for DTP professional development framework.*

## 

## **21.13 EdTech Procurement and Vendor Management** {#21.13-edtech-procurement-and-vendor-management}

### **21.13.1 Procurement Principles** {#21.13.1-procurement-principles}

Educational technology procurement follows these principles:

**Educational Value:** Primary consideration is impact on teaching and learning **Security and Privacy:** Vendors must meet data protection requirements **Total Cost of Ownership:** Consider acquisition, implementation, training, and ongoing costs **Sustainability:** Ensure long-term viability and support **Standardization:** Preference for solutions that work across the network

### **21.13.2 Procurement Process** {#21.13.2-procurement-process}

1. **Needs Assessment:** Identify educational need and requirements  
2. **Market Research:** Evaluate available options  
3. **Security Review:** Data protection and cybersecurity assessment  
4. **Pilot Testing:** Trial with limited group where appropriate  
5. **Financial Approval:** Budget authorization per procurement policy  
6. **Contract Review:** Legal review of terms and conditions  
7. **Implementation:** Deployment and training  
8. **Evaluation:** Ongoing assessment of effectiveness

### **21.13.3 Vendor Partnerships** {#21.13.3-vendor-partnerships}

Eduversal maintains partnerships with trusted vendors:

**PT. Global Zerone Digital (Zerone):** EduNav platform development and support **Bess Project (BP):** Educational equipment supply including IFPs, furniture, and technology hardware **Other Partners:** Subject to evaluation and approval processes

### **21.13.4 Data Processing Agreements** {#21.13.4-data-processing-agreements}

All vendors processing student or staff data must:

* Sign data processing agreements  
* Demonstrate compliance with Indonesian data protection requirements  
* Provide clear data handling documentation  
* Allow audit and inspection rights  
* Maintain appropriate security certifications  
* Report incidents promptly

## **21.14 Roles and Responsibilities** {#21.14-roles-and-responsibilities}

### **21.14.1 Principal** {#21.14.1-principal}

* Overall accountability for technology policy implementation  
* Approve technology-related expenditure within budget authority  
* Champion digital learning initiatives  
* Ensure staff and students comply with policies  
* Report technology matters to GM/Foundation Representative  
* Authorize emergency technology-related decisions

### **21.14.2 IT Coordinator/Manager** {#21.14.2-it-coordinator/manager}

* Day-to-day management of IT infrastructure and systems  
* User support and help desk management  
* Device management and maintenance  
* Security monitoring and incident response  
* Vendor liaison and contract management  
* Staff training coordination  
* Network and system administration  
* Policy implementation and compliance monitoring

### **21.14.3 Academic Leadership (HoDs, Subject Leaders)** {#21.14.3-academic-leadership-(hods,-subject-leaders)}

* Promote effective technology integration in teaching  
* Support teachers in using digital tools  
* Evaluate educational applications for subject areas  
* Model good practice in technology use  
* Ensure curriculum includes digital literacy where appropriate

### **21.14.4 Teachers** {#21.14.4-teachers}

* Implement technology effectively in teaching  
* Model responsible technology use  
* Monitor and manage student device use during lessons  
* Participate in technology-related professional development  
* Report technical issues and security concerns  
* Maintain EduNav/LMS with current content and grades  
* Teach digital citizenship within subject areas

### **21.14.5 Students** {#21.14.5-students}

* Follow Acceptable Use Policy  
* Use technology responsibly and ethically  
* Care for devices properly  
* Report concerns about online safety or technology issues  
* Support peers in positive technology use  
* Develop digital citizenship skills

### **21.14.6 Parents/Guardians** {#21.14.6-parents/guardians}

* Support school technology policies at home  
* Monitor student technology use outside school  
* Report concerns about online safety  
* Participate in parent education opportunities  
* Sign AUP acknowledgments annually  
* Support student in responsible device use

### **21.14.7 Eduversal Central** {#21.14.7-eduversal-central}

* Provide EduNav platform and support through Zerone partnership  
* Set network-wide technology standards  
* Coordinate procurement and vendor relationships  
* Support schools in technology planning  
* Monitor compliance through appraisal process  
* Share best practices across network

## 

## **21.15 Policy Review and Continuous Improvement** {#21.15-policy-review-and-continuous-improvement}

### **21.15.1 Annual Review Process** {#21.15.1-annual-review-process}

This section is reviewed annually:

**Timeline:**

* April-May: Gather stakeholder feedback  
* June: Analyze technology performance data  
* July: Review committee meets  
* August: Revisions finalized and approved  
* September: Updated policy communicated and implemented

**Review Committee:**

* Principal (chair)  
* IT Coordinator  
* Teacher representatives  
* Student representatives (Secondary)  
* Parent representative

### 

### **21.15.2 Inputs to Review** {#21.15.2-inputs-to-review}

**Data Analysis:**

* Technology usage data from EduNav and MDM systems  
* Security incident reports  
* Help desk ticket analysis  
* Appraisal findings on technology integration  
* Student and teacher survey results

**Stakeholder Feedback:**

* Staff survey on technology support and effectiveness  
* Student survey on technology experience  
* Parent survey on communication and access  
* Focus groups as needed

**External Factors:**

* Changes in Indonesian regulations  
* Updates to Cambridge digital requirements  
* CIS/WASC standards updates  
* Emerging technology trends and risks  
* Industry best practices

### **21.15.3 Version Control** {#21.15.3-version-control}

All updates documented with:

* Version number and date  
* Summary of changes  
* Approval signatures (Principal, GM)  
* Effective date  
* Communication plan

Previous versions retained for reference.

## 

## **21.16 Key Evidence and Artifacts for Accreditation** {#21.16-key-evidence-and-artifacts-for-accreditation}

Schools maintain the following documentation for CIS/WASC accreditation visits:

1. Digital Learning Strategy document aligned with School Improvement Plan  
2. Acceptable Use Policy (staff and student versions, bilingual) with signed acknowledgment records  
3. Technology inventory and maintenance records  
4. EduNav usage reports and system access logs  
5. Cybersecurity audit reports and vulnerability assessments  
6. Data backup and recovery test records  
7. Incident response logs and post-incident reviews  
8. AI usage guidelines and staff training records  
9. Professional development records for technology training  
10. Student and staff surveys on technology effectiveness  
11. Content filtering reports and configuration documentation  
12. Vendor contracts and data processing agreements  
13. Device management policy and MDM configuration records  
14. Digital citizenship curriculum and lesson materials

## 

## **21.17 Compliance Alignment** {#21.17-compliance-alignment}

**CIS/WASC Standards:**

* Domain D: Learning Environment and Technology Integration  
* Domain E: Educational Resources and Technology  
* Evidence of purposeful technology use to enhance learning

**Indonesian Regulations:**

* UU No. 27/2022 concerning Personal Data Protection (UU PDP)  
* Government Regulation on Electronic Systems and Transactions (PP PSTE)  
* Ministry of Communication and Information Technology (Kominfo) cybersecurity guidelines

**Cambridge Assessment:**

* Secure materials handling for examinations  
* Digital assessment guidelines  
* Academic integrity requirements related to technology and AI  
* Professional development pathways for technology integration

**Eduversal QMS:**

* EduNav platform usage requirements  
* Technology integration in EASE evaluation criteria  
* School appraisal Domain 4 indicators  
* DTP professional development alignment

## 

## **21.18 Appendices** {#21.18-appendices}

**Appendix 21A:** Acceptable Use Policy Agreement Form (Student) 

**Appendix 21B:** Acceptable Use Policy Agreement Form (Staff) 

**Appendix 21C:** Device Care Agreement Form 

**Appendix 21D:** BYOD Registration and Agreement Form 

**Appendix 21E:** Educational Application Request Form 

**Appendix 21F:** Cybersecurity Incident Report Form 

**Appendix 21G:** Data Processing Agreement Template 

**Appendix 21H:** Technology Equipment Inventory Template 

**Appendix 21I:** Digital Citizenship Curriculum Scope and Sequence 

**Appendix 21J:** AI Use Disclosure Template

##
